message.content:
Adversarially verify this finding about a design doc (the doc is at /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt; the repo is /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner; you may use WebSearch/WebFetch and read files). Try to REFUTE it. Finding from the "architecture" reviewer, severity critical:
TITLE: No ephemeral lifecycle, crash recovery, or orphan/disk cleanup
DETAIL: 'Ephemeral runners by default' is stated as a core principle, but nothing in the design destroys a VM after a job, recovers from a host crash mid-job, garbage-collects orphaned VMs/containers, or reclaims disk. src/runner.rs stop() is a stub, and there is no state store recording what was started — so `stop` cannot even enumerate what to kill after a restart of the CLI. Disk exhaustion is the single most common self-hosted runner failure mode (dangling VM disks, docker layer buildup, workspace/_work directories, tool caches), and the design has zero disk reclamation, zero disk limits, and zero orphan reaper. A crash between VM-create and job-complete permanently leaks a 4G-RAM/multi-GB-disk VM with no mechanism to notice.
RECOMMENDATION: Add a state file (e.g. $XDG_STATE_HOME/gha-isolated/state.json) recording every provisioned VM/container with labels/ownership tags; tag guests at the hypervisor level (Tart VM name prefix, libvirt metadata, Docker labels) so a reaper can find orphans without the state file; define a `gha-isolated gc` that runs on start and on a timer under the service manager; specify post-job destroy semantics (who watches for job completion — outrunner or the wrapper); and add disk high-water-mark checks that stop accepting jobs before the host fills.
Is the finding factually correct and does the recommendation make sense? If the factual basis is wrong or the severity is inflated, refute it. Default to refuted=true only if you have concrete contrary evidence or the claim is unsupported after checking.

content.content:
2:Here is a complete, standalone design and Rust skeleton for gha-isolated — a wrapper CLI around gha-outrunner that gives you unified, reliable, VM-preferring GitHub Actions runners across macOS, Linux, and GCE.
10:	•	Use GitHub Scale Sets + ephemeral runners by default.
12:	•	Work the same way on a developer laptop and inside a GCE VM.
16:	•	Linux / GCE (no strong KVM) → Docker + Sysbox (with strict limits)
17:	•	GCE → Same as Linux (run inside a GCE VM)
27:└── Optional GCE helpers
51:Linux / GCE
284:On GCE: Create a Linux VM → cargo install gha-isolated (or copy binary) → run the same commands.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "gha-outrunner GitHub Actions runner ephemeral VM tart libvirt"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner: Ephemeral VM and container runners for GitHub Actions — no Kubernetes required","url":"https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner"},{"title":"gha-outrunner/docs/howto/custom-tart-macos-image.md at main · NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner","url":"https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner/blob/main/docs/howto/custom-tart-macos-image.md"},{"title":"Enable nested virtualization · actions runner-images","url":"https://github.com/actions/runner-images/discussions/7191"},{"title":"tart · GitHub Topics · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/topics/tart"},{"title":"Self-hosted runners reference - GitHub Docs","url":"https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/runners/self-hosted-runners"},{"title":"Ephemeral GHA Apple Silicon Runners with CiderMill","url":"https://langui.sh/2023/01/15/ephemeral-gha-m1-runners-with-cidermill/"},{"title":"Namespace: Running GitHub Self-Hosted Runners Reliably","url":"https://namespace.so/blog/running-github-self-hosted-runners-reliably"},{"title":"GitHub - mirego/ekiden: GitHub Actions self-hosted arm64 macOS runners · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/mirego/ekiden"},{"title":"GitHub Actions: Ephemeral self-hosted runners and new webhooks for auto-scaling | Hacker News","url":"https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=28601618"},{"title":"Create Ephemeral Self-Hosted Runners for GitHub Actions | Thomas Stringer","url":"https://trstringer.com/create-ephemeral-self-hosted-runners-github-actions/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about gha-outrunner and the related technologies:

## gha-outrunner

gha-outrunner provides ephemeral VM and container runners for GitHub Actions without requiring Kubernetes. It's designed for users running on bare metal or a simple VPS who shouldn't need a cluster just to get ephemeral runners, and it gives isolation guarantees with Docker, libvirt, or Tart.

## Supported Virtualization Backends

The project supports multiple virtualization options:
- **Docker** - Container-based runners
- **libvirt** - Linux virtualization platform
- **Tart** - Specialized for macOS virtualization

## macOS with Tart

For macOS, gha-outrunner can use Tart with configuration options like specifying the image, runner command, CPUs, and memory, with changes persisting to the base image while outrunner jobs use clones.

## Related Projects

CiderMill was created to provide ephemeral macOS ARM64 runners and uses Tart and GitHub Actions' ephemeral runner support to provide build isolation. Additionally, other projects use Tart to orchestrate virtual machines for GitHub Actions self-hosted runners.

The key advantage of ephemeral runners is that GitHub only assigns one job to an ephemeral runner, allowing you to manage runners as ephemeral systems with a clean environment for each job, which helps limit exposure of sensitive resources and mitigates risk from compromised runners.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Does gha-outrunner destroy VMs/containers after each job (ephemeral lifecycle)? Does it handle crash recovery, orphaned VM cleanup / garbage collection, or disk reclamation? Quote relevant README sections about lifecycle, cleanup, ephemeral behavior, and any gc/reaper features.

content.content:
# gha-outrunner: Ephemeral Lifecycle & Cleanup

Based on the README content provided, here's what's documented about VM/container lifecycle:

## Ephemeral Behavior

Yes, gha-outrunner is designed for ephemeral execution. The README states: **"outrunner provisions fresh Docker containers or VMs for each GitHub Actions job, then destroys them when the job completes."**

## Cleanup & Garbage Collection

However, the provided README excerpt does **not contain explicit documentation** about:
- Crash recovery mechanisms
- Orphaned VM cleanup procedures
- Garbage collection / reaper features
- Disk reclamation strategies

The README directs users to more detailed documentation:
- Reference guides on [provisioner lifecycle details](https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner/blob/main/docs/reference/provisioners.md)
- [Architecture documentation](https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner/blob/main/docs/explanation/architecture.md)

**Recommendation**: For comprehensive information on crash recovery, orphaned resource cleanup, and garbage collection, you'd need to review the linked documentation files or the project's detailed reference guides, which aren't included in the page content provided.

content.text: The core delegated tool already destroys VMs per job. Let me check whether it also handles crash recovery/orphan cleanup.

input.prompt: Does gha-outrunner handle orphaned VM/container cleanup, crash recovery after host or process restart, garbage collection of leftover VMs, or disk reclamation? Quote any relevant sections about cleanup, teardown, orphans, naming/labels of VMs/containers, or startup reconciliation.

content.content:
# gha-outrunner Cleanup & Recovery Handling

## Orphaned Container/VM Cleanup

**Docker:** The tool creates containers with `"AutoRemove: true"`, enabling automatic cleanup when the runner exits. However, if outrunner is terminated unexpectedly, orphaned containers may persist. The documentation notes: `"Remove them with docker ps -a --filter label=outrunner"`. Containers use labels `outrunner=true` and `outrunner.name=<name>` for identification.

**Libvirt:** On startup, outrunner performs reconciliation by listing all domains and destroying any whose names match the runner's scale set prefix, plus corresponding overlay files.

**Tart:** Similarly, at startup the tool runs `"tart list --quiet"` and deletes VMs matching the scale set name prefix via `tart stop` and `tart delete`.

## Naming & Identification

- **Docker:** Containers are `"Named using the scale set name as prefix (e.g., linux-a1b2c3d4)"` with identifying labels
- **Libvirt/Tart:** VMs use scale set name prefixes for startup-time cleanup identification

## Disk Reclamation

Libvirt's overlay directory can be configured via `LibvirtConfig.OverlayDir` to manage ephemeral disk usage. The documentation provides no details on automatic garbage collection or disk quota management across provisioners.

The documentation does not address crash recovery mechanisms, persistent state recovery, or host restart scenarios beyond the startup reconciliation patterns described.

input.reasoning: The finding's factual basis collapses because it ignores that the design is explicitly a thin wrapper around gha-outrunner (doc lines 2, 25: "Runner Lifecycle (start/stop/status via gha-outrunner)"), and gha-outrunner (real tool, NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner) already implements the lifecycle the finding claims is absent: (1) its README states it "provisions fresh Docker containers or VMs for each GitHub Actions job, then destroys them when the job completes" — so post-job destroy semantics exist and are owned by outrunner, answering the recommendation's open question; (2) its provisioners doc documents startup reconciliation that reaps orphans after a crash — libvirt lists all domains and destroys any matching the scale-set prefix plus overlay files, Tart does `tart list` + `tart stop`/`tart delete` on matching VMs — so a crash mid-job does NOT "permanently leak a VM with no mechanism to notice" once the service manager (which the design includes) restarts the process; (3) guests are already tagged at the hypervisor level (Docker labels outrunner=true/outrunner.name, scale-set name prefixes for libvirt/Tart VMs), which is exactly the finding's own recommended mitigation, making the "no state store, stop cannot enumerate" claim a misdiagnosis — enumeration is label/prefix-based by design. (4) stop() being a stub is true but the doc self-identifies as skeleton code with "Full runner.rs implementation" as explicit next step #1; stub-ness of admitted placeholder code cannot carry a critical architecture finding. Residual valid points are real but medium severity at most: the doc should state the delegation assumption explicitly; Docker-backend orphans after unclean outrunner death need manual label-filtered cleanup; and neither layer has disk high-water-mark checks or quota gc (though per-job guest destruction is itself the primary disk reclamation mechanism, contradicting "zero disk reclamation"). Severity "critical" is inflated and three of the four core factual claims are contradicted by the wrapped tool's documentation.
input.corrected_claim: The design delegates ephemeral lifecycle to gha-outrunner, which already destroys each VM/container after its job, tags guests at the hypervisor level (Docker labels, scale-set name prefixes), and reaps orphaned libvirt/Tart VMs via startup reconciliation — so post-job destroy, orphan cleanup, and crash recovery are not absent, just externally owned. Remaining gaps (medium severity): the design doc never states this delegation assumption explicitly; Docker-backend containers orphaned by an unclean outrunner death require manual `docker ps --filter label=outrunner` cleanup; the wrapper's stop()/status() are unimplemented skeleton stubs; and neither layer has disk high-water-mark checks or quota-based garbage collection of base images/overlays, so a disk-pressure gate before accepting jobs is a worthwhile v1 hardening addition.
